programs.name: DenaliCare
programs.tagline: If your parent needs daily care with bathing, dressing, or medications, Alaska Medicaid may cover home services or nursing home care.

localEntities.name: Division of Senior and Disability Services offices
Local Aging and Disability Resource Centers (ADRCs)

programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+ and needs daily help with basic tasks like bathing, dressing, or managing medications, DenaliCare (Alaska's Medicaid program) may cover the full cost of long-term care services. This includes personal care in their home through Home and Community Based Services (HCBS) waivers, or full nursing home care if needed. The program also covers home modifications if your parent can't live safely without them.

To qualify, your parent must meet strict financial limits: income under $2,982/month for a single person and assets under $2,000. But equally important, they must pass a Nursing Facility Level of Care assessment (NFLOC) — a clinical evaluation proving they need the same level of daily assistance provided in a nursing home, even if they want to stay at home.

DenaliCare is distinct from Denali KidCare (Alaska's children's program). For married couples, spousal protection rules may allow higher income and asset limits to prevent impoverishing the healthy spouse.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must need daily help with basic activities like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, or mobility, plus may need help with cooking, cleaning, shopping, or managing medications

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online through Alaska's ARIES portal, by phone at 800-478-7778, or at your local Division of Public Assistance office.

steps.title: Contact your local Aging and Disability Resource Center (ADRC)
steps.description: They'll help determine if DenaliCare is right for your parent and prepare the required document list.

steps.title: Complete Application for Services (GEN 50C)
steps.description: Submit online via ARIES portal, by phone, or mail to your local Division of Public Assistance office.

steps.title: Provide financial and medical documentation
steps.description: Include proof of income, assets, residency, and medical records showing need for daily care assistance.

steps.title: Complete NFLOC assessment
steps.description: A clinical evaluator will assess your parent's need for nursing facility level care, even if applying for home services.

applicationGuide.tip: Start with your local ADRC — they can guide you through the complex eligibility requirements and help prepare your application properly.

tiers.name: HCBS Waivers
tiers.description: Personal care, home modifications, and support services to help your parent stay at home
tiers.coverage: Services needed to avoid nursing home placement

tiers.name: Nursing Home Medicaid
tiers.description: Full coverage of nursing home care when home care isn't sufficient
tiers.coverage: All nursing home costs after NFLOC qualification

tiers.name: ABD Medicaid
tiers.description: Basic Medicaid plus long-term care services for aged, blind, or disabled individuals
tiers.coverage: Medical care plus approved long-term care services

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
excluded: Burial plots and small burial funds

categories.name: Identity & Residency
items: Alaska driver's license or state ID

categories.name: Medical
items: Recent medical records showing care needs
Doctor's assessment of daily living assistance needs

contentSections.text: Don't confuse DenaliCare (for seniors needing long-term care) with Denali KidCare (for children). The programs have completely different eligibility rules and benefits.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for DenaliCare?
faqs.answer: Yes, your parent can usually keep their primary home under DenaliCare's asset limits, as long as the home equity stays under Alaska's limits. The home is considered an exempt asset for Medicaid purposes.

faqs.question: What if Dad's income is just over the $2,982 limit?
faqs.answer: Income limits vary by program and marital status, and spousal impoverishment protections may apply if your parents are married. There may also be planning strategies to qualify even if over limits — consult with an elder law attorney or your local ADRC for guidance.

faqs.question: Does DenaliCare cover home modifications like ramps or grab bars?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the modifications are necessary for your parent to live safely at home and avoid nursing home placement, DenaliCare may cover home modifications through HCBS waivers.

faqs.question: How do they determine if Mom needs 'nursing facility level of care'?
faqs.answer: A clinical evaluator will assess your parent's ability to perform Activities of Daily Living (bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, mobility) and Instrumental Activities (cooking, cleaning, shopping, managing medications). They also evaluate cognitive and behavioral issues that affect safety.

faqs.question: Can Dad get DenaliCare services if he wants to stay home instead of going to a nursing home?
faqs.answer: Yes, that's exactly what HCBS waivers are designed for. If your parent qualifies for nursing facility level of care but wants to stay home, DenaliCare can provide personal care services and support to make that possible.

faqs.question: If Mom is married, do they count Dad's income and assets too?
faqs.answer: Yes, but Alaska has spousal impoverishment protections that may allow your parents to keep more income and assets than the standard limits. The rules vary significantly based on which spouse is applying and their specific situation.

programs.name: Adults with Physical & Developmental Disabilities Waiver (APDD)
programs.tagline: If your parent has a developmental disability and needs nursing home-level care, this waiver can help them stay at home instead.

localEntities.name: SDS Intellectual and Developmental Disabilities Unit; regional ADRC sites (e.g., Juneau, Ketchikan); local care coordinators

programs.intro:
If your parent is 21+ with a developmental disability like autism or intellectual disability and needs nursing home-level care, Alaska's APDD Waiver can help them receive services at home or in assisted living instead. The program covers medical and supportive services individualized to your parent's needs, allowing them to live in their community rather than a nursing facility.

This isn't for typical aging issues — your parent must have a diagnosed developmental disability AND need nursing facility level of care (daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or mobility). The state assesses both requirements through separate evaluations. Because this is a Medicaid waiver program, your parent must also meet Alaska's Medicaid income and resource limits, determined through an interview with the Division of Public Assistance.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must need nursing facility level of care (daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or mobility) as assessed by the state
otherRequirements: Diagnosed developmental disability (autism, intellectual disability, or similar condition)

applicationGuide.summary: Contact a local care coordinator who will arrange both the developmental disability assessment and Medicaid financial eligibility interview — processing time not specified.

steps.title: Call your regional care coordinator
steps.description: Contact your local ADRC (Aging and Disability Resource Center) or care coordinator to start the process. They will coordinate both assessments you need.

steps.title: Complete developmental disability assessment
steps.description: The SDS Intellectual and Developmental Disabilities Unit will assess whether your parent has a qualifying developmental disability and needs nursing facility level of care.

steps.title: Apply for Medicaid financial eligibility
steps.description: The Division of Public Assistance (DPA) will interview your parent and review income/resources to determine Medicaid eligibility.

steps.title: Receive eligibility determination
steps.description: Get a letter confirming eligibility. If approved, services will be individualized based on your parent's assessment.

applicationGuide.tip: Start with the care coordinator — they handle coordinating between the different agencies so you don't have to navigate multiple systems alone.

categories.name: Medical
items: Medical records showing need for daily care assistance

categories.name: Financial
items: Proof of income (Social Security statements, pension records)
Any other financial records requested by DPA for Medicaid eligibility

contentSections.text: This waiver is specifically for developmental disabilities — not typical aging issues or physical disabilities alone. If your parent is 65+ without a developmental disability, look into Alaska's ALI Waiver instead.
You need both approvals: developmental disability assessment AND Medicaid financial eligibility. Being over the income limit will result in automatic denial, so check Medicaid limits first.

faqs.question: What's the difference between APDD and ALI waivers?
faqs.answer: APDD is specifically for people with developmental disabilities who need nursing home-level care. ALI is for people 65+ or adults 21-64 with physical disabilities, but doesn't require a developmental disability diagnosis.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house if they qualify?
faqs.answer: Generally yes — the home is typically exempt from Medicaid resource limits. The Division of Public Assistance will review your parent's specific situation during the financial eligibility interview.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is just over the Medicaid limit?
faqs.answer: They won't qualify for the waiver. However, income limits can be complex, so have DPA review the situation — some income may not count, or there may be deductions that help them qualify.

faqs.question: How do I prove my parent has a developmental disability?
faqs.answer: You'll need medical documentation of the diagnosis (autism, intellectual disability, etc.). The SDS Intellectual and Developmental Disabilities Unit will review records and may conduct their own assessment.

faqs.question: What services does the waiver actually cover?
faqs.answer: Services are individualized based on assessment, but can include personal care, respite care, day services, and other supports to help your parent live at home or in assisted living instead of a nursing facility.

faqs.question: What if there's no care coordinator in my area?
faqs.answer: The program is statewide, but you may need to work with coordinators in larger cities. Call the main Alaska HCBS line or try the Southeast Alaska ADRC in Juneau at 907-523-4428 for guidance.

programs.name: Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)
programs.tagline: If your parent needs nursing home-level care, PACE could provide all their medical and social services in one program — but it's not available in Alaska.

localEntities.name: No PACE providers in Alaska; check National PACE Association for updates

programs.intro:
If your parent is 55+ and needs significant daily help — but you want them to stay home instead of moving to a nursing home — PACE provides everything under one roof. One team manages all their medical care, prescriptions, transportation, personal care, and social services with no deductibles or copays. Because PACE covers nursing home-level care in the community, your parent gets the intensive support they need while staying in familiar surroundings.

The challenge: No PACE programs operate in Alaska. This comprehensive program exists in select areas across the country, but Alaska residents cannot access it regardless of how well they meet other eligibility requirements. If you're considering relocation to access PACE, most participants are dual-eligible for Medicare and Medicaid, though no income or asset limits apply.

summary: Must live in PACE service area
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be certified by Alaska as meeting Nursing Facility Level of Care (daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or similar intensive support needs)
otherRequirements: Live in the service area of a PACE organization (none in Alaska)
Able to live safely in the community with PACE services
Not enrolled in Medicare Advantage, Medicare Part D, or hospice

applicationGuide.summary: Contact Medicare at 1-800-633-4227 to discuss PACE options outside Alaska, but no Alaska applications are possible.

steps.title: Confirm no Alaska availability
steps.description: Call Medicare at 1-800-633-4227 or check the National PACE Association website to verify no Alaska programs exist

steps.title: Research relocation options
steps.description: If considering moving, identify PACE service areas in other states where your parent might relocate

steps.title: Get NFLOC assessment
steps.description: Obtain Nursing Facility Level of Care certification from Alaska before potential relocation

steps.title: Contact PACE organization
steps.description: If relocating, contact the specific PACE provider in your target area to begin enrollment process

applicationGuide.processingTime: Enrollment effective first day of month after signed agreement received
applicationGuide.waitlist: Common due to limited geographic availability; many areas have waitlists
applicationGuide.tip: If your parent needs this level of care, explore Alaska's Medicaid home and community-based waiver programs as alternatives.

contentSections.text: No PACE programs operate in Alaska. This program is only available in select service areas nationwide, and Alaska residents cannot access it regardless of eligibility.

contentSections.heading: What You'd Need (If Moving to a PACE Area)

categories.name: Identity & Residency
items: Proof of residence in PACE service area

categories.name: Medical
items: Nursing Facility Level of Care certification

contentSections.text: If not Medicaid-eligible, private pay premiums for the long-term care portion average $4,000-$5,000/month. Most PACE participants qualify for both Medicare and Medicaid.

faqs.question: Can my parent access PACE if we're willing to relocate from Alaska?
faqs.answer: Yes, but you'd need to establish residency in a PACE service area first. Your parent would need to live within the specific geographic boundaries of a PACE organization and meet their Nursing Facility Level of Care requirements.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's income is too high for Medicaid but they need PACE services?
faqs.answer: PACE has no income limits, but without Medicaid, your parent pays private premiums averaging $4,000-$5,000/month for the long-term care portion. Medicare still covers the medical services.

faqs.question: Can my parent see their current doctors if they enroll in PACE?
faqs.answer: No. PACE participants must use only PACE providers and cannot see outside doctors or specialists. The PACE team becomes their sole source of medical care.

faqs.question: What if my parent's care needs decrease after enrolling in PACE?
faqs.answer: PACE enrollment continues until death, regardless of changing needs. It's an irrevocable commitment to the program, so your parent would remain enrolled even if their condition improves.

faqs.question: Are there Alaska alternatives that provide similar comprehensive care?
faqs.answer: Alaska offers Medicaid home and community-based waiver programs that can provide some similar services, though not the all-inclusive model of PACE. Contact Alaska's Medicaid office to explore these alternatives.

faqs.question: How long are the waitlists for PACE programs in other states?
faqs.answer: Waitlists vary significantly by location and are common due to limited capacity. Some areas have months-long waits, while others may have immediate openings. Contact specific PACE organizations directly for current wait times.
